title: How to prevent a rewrite
description:
When working on a constantly changing corporate codebase for many years, you run the risk of falling behind. Techdebt keeps rising. You experience framework lock-in, vendor lock-in and you cannot try out the new fun technology that all other developers seems to talk about.

How can you achieve keeping your project, code and tech stack clean, always-on, up to date, secure and fun?

title: Engineering Leadership through a Social Network Lens
description:
From organization charts to reporting lines, engineering organizations are often described and understood through top-down hierarchies. In practice, engineering and the collaboration it requires flows through something else entirely. Code reviews, Slack mentions, task handoffs, and informal collaborations form a living, breathing network that demonstrates how teams actually operate.

In this talk, I share a two-year, real-world case study of what happened when I applied Social Network Thinking to my engineering team. By paying attention to patterns of connection, I developed a clearer picture of onboarding dynamics, senior and leadership bottlenecks, and moments where people may need additional support alongside our existing management practices.

I walk through concrete interventions that emerged from viewing the team as a network rather than as a hierarchy. These include junior-only task forces, mob programming sessions, and intentionally reshaping collaboration patterns to reduce over-reliance on a small number of senior engineers. I also argue that Social Network Analysis is an exploratory tool not a performance metric. Measures like connectedness or centrality are signals that benefit from context, curiosity, and follow-up through 1-1s and coaching, not scoring or ranking individuals.

Finally, I show how engineering leaders can explore their own team networks using lightweight tooling, simple note-taking practices, and careful observation. Social Network Thinking is a practical, applicable heuristic that complements time-tested leadership tools and helps software leaders reason more clearly about how their teams work.

title: Stop Chat-and-Hope and Start Building Better Apps with Agents
description:
AI coding assistants have evolved from simple autocomplete to autonomous agents, yet many developers remain trapped in ""Vibe Coding""—a cycle of endless prompt-tweaking that results in unmaintainable ""Spaghetti Monster"" codebases. As we move into 2025, engineering teams responsible for production uptime can no longer rely on hallucinated solutions built without a plan. To scale effectively, we must move beyond the ""chat-and-hope"" loop and mature our workflow from reactive chatting to proactive orchestration, turning AI from an unpredictable oracle into a governed engineering tool.

This session provides a roadmap for this transition, starting with the foundations of AI governance and connectivity. We will explore how to create rules and guidelines to enforce architectural patterns, use Model Context Protocol (MCP) to grant agents secure tool access, and use advanced prompting to decouple intent from implementation. With all of these in place, we will learn to use Spec-Driven Development (SDD), to build production level features. You will see all of them through a live demonstration of building a production-grade fullstack application, you will see how to act as the driver—defining the spec and constraints—while the agent handles the heavy lifting of execution, testing, and refactoring under your full control as the tool it is.

title: Engineering Productivity Starts with Predictable Behaviour
description:
Srini V Srinivasan is a Founder and Chief Technology Officer at Aerospike and will speak about how Engineering Productivity Starts with Predictable Behaviour.

Engineering teams chase productivity through tools, frameworks, and headcount, but the real drag on delivery is often unpredictable behaviour in production systems. Latency spikes, partial failures, and “rare” edge cases quietly tax engineers with firefighting, context switching, and defensive over‑engineering.

This talk shows how global leaders like PayPal, Rakuten, Adobe, and Airtel reach internet‑scale traffic with small, highly productive teams by insisting on predictable behaviour in the data layer: tight p99 latency budgets, bounded fan‑out, and architectures that stay online through failures. Attendees will leave with a practical checklist they can apply to their own stacks to turn “it usually works” infrastructure into a platform their engineers can trust and build on quickly.

title: Real-world experience with self-hosted GIS solutions, based on open geo data
description: Case Study of Auto1 Group with adoption of OSM-based geo-services and using of self-hosted solutions as an alternative of cloud services like Google Maps API.

title: The New Frontend Stack: Humans, AI, and Prompts
description:
Modern frontend development is rapidly changing with the rise of AI-assisted coding tools. Developers are no longer the only ones writing code — large language models are becoming active participants in the development workflow. This introduces a new layer in the frontend stack: prompts. Instead of writing every line manually, developers increasingly describe intent through prompts that generate UI components, API integrations, and application logic.

﻿But this shift raises important engineering questions: How do we design systems when part of the code is generated by AI?

How do we debug non-deterministic code? What new role does the developer play in this architecture? In this talk we explore the emerging frontend stack: Humans, AI, and Prompts, examine how AI tools reshape development workflows, and discuss practical patterns for integrating AI into production-grade engineering.

title: Giving AI Agents a Wallet
description: HTTP 402 has been in the spec since 1997 and largely ignored, because the agents that would need it didn't exist yet. They do now. This talk walks through a working implementation of the “Payment Required” status code, covering how requests are structured, how payment negotiation works, and what it looks like in practice when an agent pays for content. You'll leave with a clear picture of how the protocol works and where it fits.

title: Multiplayer Game Dynamics: Behind The Scenes Of the New York Times' new Game: CrossPlay
description:
Shafik Quoraishee from The New York Times with an AI Frontier Research Session!

The New York Times Games team presents a technical breakdown of CrossPlay, a real-time multiplayer word game built as a standalone mobile app rather than on a traditional game engine. The session explains how CrossPlay's architecture supports fast matchmaking, responsive gameplay, and large-scale social interaction using native mobile frameworks. Speakers walk through the platform's guiding product pillars—Let Them Play, Learn & Grow, and Smarter Social—and how those principles translate into concrete engineering decisions.

Topics include fanout-based real-time systems, performance-driven UI design, AI-assisted learning tools, and a production-ready automated content moderation service. Attendees gain insight into how disciplined release processes, data-informed iteration, and trust-focused systems enable a modern multiplayer experience that balances speed, depth, and safety at scale

Takeaway
Attendees learn how to design real-time multiplayer systems using fanout architectures, manage performance as a user-facing constraint, integrate AI-driven player feedback tools, and roll out moderated social features safely. Concrete examples include matchmaking limits, stateless moderation services, and native mobile interaction patterns.

Intended Audience
Attendees learn how clear engagement pillars guide system design, from fast game loops and moderated social play. They gain practical approaches for tying real-time infrastructure, coaching tools, and trust systems directly to retention, progression, and long-term player value..

Podcast Link: https://schedule.gdconf.com/speaker/quoraishee-shafik.77460

In this live podcast session, dive into the world of mobile gaming with Amra Khullar and Shafik Quoraishee from The New York Times. They discuss the development of Crossplay, NYT’s latest game, and share insights into what it takes to succeed in today’s mobile market.

Takeaway
Attendees will gain valuable insights into mobile game development and strategies for creating successful products in a competitive market.

title: Sustainable IT The Path to a Greener Future
description:
In our perpetual quest for the holy grail of technology, we would be remiss if we do not consider the adverse impact on sustainability factors. This keynote focuses on IT sustainability, what actions we can take to develop sustainable IT solutions and the urgency to act on this!

Why do we need to acknowledge Sustainable IT and Green Software

What is Green Software and how to we build expertise around it

Where is the impact most felt when we follow best practices around sustainable IT.

title: How Great Tech Teams Build a Productivity Culture That Scales
description:
As tech teams grow, productivity often declines not because people work less, but because engineering practices, workflows, and decision paths no longer scale. Teams introduce more tools, processes, and handoffs, yet delivery slows and focus is lost.

Great tech teams take a different approach.

In this talk, we’ll explore how high-performing engineering teams build a productivity culture that scales through clear ownership, strong technical foundations, and fast feedback loops. Instead of relying on ever-expanding tool stacks, these teams focus on how work flows through the system to reduce friction and enable consistent, high-quality delivery.

title: Agentic AI Meets Event-Driven Architecture: Practical Solutions for Enterprise Challenges
description:
Agentic AI is exciting and full of promise, but the adoption road can be bumpy. Teams often get stuck wrestling with messy data, complex integrations, tricky security and compliance requirements, unpredictable costs, and the challenge of keeping everything transparent and manageable.

In this session, we’ll explore how event-driven architectures (EDA) can help smooth out these bumps. By connecting agents asynchronously and letting them communicate through event streams, we can simplify integration, enforce good governance, and make it much easier to scale and observe what’s happening in real time. This approach reduces unnecessary data shuffling, keeps compliance in check, and gives teams the flexibility to adapt as needs change.

We’ll share real-world examples—from supply chain automation to customer support—to show how EDA principles (and, as an example, solutions like Solace Agent Mesh) can turn agentic AI challenges into manageable, even empowering, opportunities for innovation.

title: Risk, Evidence and Conformity. The Cyber Resilience Act for the rest of us
description:
Daniel Yvetot, Founder of Tauri about Risk, Evidence and Conformity. The Cyber Resilience Act for the rest of us.

In this session, we dive into the Product Liability Directive and Cyber Resilience Act with Daniel Thompson, CEO of Crab Nebula and founder of Tauri. The EU’s new legislative framework impacts manufacturers in ways we don’t totally understand, but are going to bring substantial changes to how companies use and develop open source. Daniel explains the broader implications for software security and the future of digital products in the European market.

title: The Formula for Faster Outage Recovery
description:
Production outages can be extremely costly. For a global business, just one minute of downtime can exceed an engineer’s annual compensation. So, how can engineering leaders strengthen incident response across their organizations?

After over a decade of building and operating systems used by millions of people, I’ve distilled an approach that helps engineering leaders strengthen their teams’ incident management practices, based on a simple formula:

Outage Duration = Time to Detect + Time to Acknowledge + Time to Repair.

To resolve outages quickly, we need to be efficient in all three stages. But shortening the time of each stage requires a coordinated mix of technical, process, and cultural changes. This is where engineering leaders can truly enable and empower their teams.

We’ll unpack each component and examine practical strategies—from tooling investments and observability practices to cultural habits and on-call readiness—that can dramatically shorten outage duration. By the end of the talk, you’ll learn how to set up your teams for success when they face an outage.

title: How can we abuse AI hallucinations to feed the already bloated software supply chain
description:
Generative AI has already become that semi-autonomous software developer in our pockets, that’s a reality for almost all of us in tech teams. However, as Uncle Ben would say, “with great power comes great responsibility”.

The tool that is providing us with benefits in software development is simultaneously responsible for creating a whole new software supply chain risk called slopsquatting. Similar to typosquatting, slopsquatting exploits AI’s tendency to hallucinate.

﻿By suggesting non-existent software packages from public repositories like PyPi or DockerHub, hackers are coming up with new and creative ways to distribute malware. An adversary monitoring these “phantom” packages can register them with malicious payloads, turning a harmless AI mistake into a silent supply chain compromise. This talk explores how slopsquatting works in the wild, accompanied by a live demo of the attack chain, and strategies for defending against it. If your build pipeline trusts public upstreams, I’m afraid your trusty AI companion might just be doing the recon for your attacker.

title: It passed auth, then production caught fire
description:
Most teams still think of authorization as a security concern. In modern systems, it has quietly become a reliability concern as well.

Authorization logic is no longer confined to one codebase. It is spread across application services, API gateways, background workers, data platforms, and now AI driven automation. Each layer makes its own decision, often with incomplete context and slightly different assumptions. This works until scale, automation, and non human identities turn small inconsistencies into systemic failure.

In this talk, we will look at real world failure modes where authorization bugs manifested as cascading outages, stuck workflows, data corruption, or unexplainable behavior under load. We will show how fragmentation hides

title: Building Trustworthy AI: Authentication & Authorization for AI Agents with Auth0
description: AI agents are rapidly evolving from simple chatbots into powerful, autonomous assistants that can perform complex tasks on our behalf. This fundamental shift in software presents incredible opportunities, but also exposes massive security and identity challenges. How can we trust these agents with our sensitive data and empower them to act securely? In this talk we will demonstrate how a robust identity layer is the key to building trustworthy AI. You'll learn practical approaches for authenticating users in AI-driven apps, securely managing API access via a Token Vault, implementing human-in-the-loop for user consent, and enforcing fine-grained authorization within RAG pipelines to ensure agents only access the data they're allowed to.

title: The Inbox Is an Agent Runtime: Why Email Is the Best Protocol We Have for AI Agents
description: Every agent demo is a chat window. It shouldn't be. Email is async, universal, and stateful — exactly what agents need. I'll show you how to build an agent that lives at an email address, remembers users, and works from a single platform. Live demo on stage

title: AST + AI = Developer Co-Pilot 2.0
description:
How code understanding and automation can transform your team’s and clients productivity.

What if your codebase could improve itself?

In this talk, we’ll explore how combining Abstract Syntax Trees (AST) with AI can turn your development workflow into a self-evolving system. While AI tools like Copilot help us write code faster, they still lack a deep understanding of structure and context. ASTs, on the other hand, can precisely navigate, analyze, and transform code — safely and automatically.

By merging these two forces, we can build intelligent automation that doesn’t just suggest improvements but actually applies them — from API migrations and performance optimizations to automated instrumentation and observability hooks.

I’ll share how our team uses AST-based transformations and AI-driven insights to eliminate repetitive work, speed up code reviews, and let engineers focus on solving real problems instead of chasing syntax.

You’ll walk away with:

A practical understanding of how ASTs can power AI-driven code automation
Real examples of self-updating and self-healing code
Strategies to integrate these ideas into your team’s productivity toolkit

title: How to feed your AI agents with data
description:
How to feed your AI agents with data Workshop by Kevin Lewis from Apify.

AI agents are only as useful as the data and tools they can access. In this workshop, you'll learn how to give your AI agents superpowers by connecting them to real-world web data using the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and Apify's Agent Skills.

We'll walk through how MCP works and how Apify uses it to let AI agents access thousands of ready-made web scraping and data extraction tools. Then you'll get hands-on. You'll connect an AI agent to Apify's MCP server, explore existing Agent Skills, and write your own custom skill that fetches live data from the web.

What you'll do:

Set up and use Apify's MCP server with an AI agent
Browse and use existing Agent Skills for web data extraction
Write your own Agent

Skill from scratch

No prior experience with MCP or Apify needed.

Please bring your laptop with an AI coding tool installed (e.g. Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, or similar).

title: Fireside with CEO of Gitbook: Future of Documentation with AI
description:
Join this Fireside Chat with the CEO of Gitbook Sara Tandowsky about the Future of Documentation in the Era of AI.

 • Why documentation fails in most orgs

 • Moving from static docs → dynamic knowledge systems

 • Docs as part of the product, not an afterthought

 • How AI changes expectations

title: Culture at Speed: Scaling Without Losing People in the AI era
description: Some of Europe’s most exciting scale-ups have been built by betting big on culture. In this session, we share how to grow a high-performing team and organisation by putting people at the centre, through chaos, growth hacks, and expansion sprints—while maintaining team effectiveness and delivery. Expect real talk on what it takes to lead with heart, build motivated teams, and scale without breaking your culture.

title: Automating Accessibility Testing with Playwright
description: Accessibility is often treated as a manual checklist or a one‑off audit — which means issues are found late, or not at all. In this talk, we’ll show how accessibility can become a natural part of your automated testing pipeline. Based on real‑world experience building and maintaining large‑scale web platforms, we’ll demonstrate how Playwright can be used to catch common accessibility issues early, alongside your functional tests. You’ll see practical examples that connect WCAG principles to real code, covering topics like semantics, keyboard navigation and usability. The focus is on practical, developer‑friendly automation you can apply immediately — an approach we also use at Nationale‑Nederlanden to make accessibility a shared, continuous responsibility within engineering teams.

title: The AI-First Playbook: Building and Running a Business with AI
Building and Deploying a Multi-Agent AI D&D with TypeScript
description:
Ever wondered what happens when you give multiple AI agents different roles and let them collaborate? In this session, we'll build a multi-agent system that creates and runs a Dungeons & Dragons campaign.

We'll create an AI ""Dungeon Master's Guild"" using the Swarm pattern: a Dungeon Master storyteller, World Builder for locations and NPCs, Combat Director for tactical encounters, and Quest Weaver for story arcs. Each agent autonomously hands off to the right specialist through Strands' coordination tools.

We'll explore how MCP servers act as magical tools - dice rollers, monster manuals, and spell compendiums that agents discover dynamically. You'll see live character creation with audience input, real-time world building with shared context, AI agents conversing via autonomous handoffs, and a complete D&D session where the audience becomes the adventuring party.

Learn multi-agent architecture with Strands' orchestration patterns (Graph, Swarm, Workflow), TypeScript patterns using tools, MCP integration, and best practices for orchestrating multiple AI models.

No D&D experience required - just bring your curiosity for AI and love of TypeScript!

title: How to Structure Context to Make Your Agents Smarter
description:
When agents fail at complex tasks, the problem is rarely the model itself— it's the lack of a proper context that is required for complex reasoning. Retrieving similar documents isn't enough when agents need to understand how entities relate, how knowledge connects across domains, or how information chains together to answer multi-step questions.

In this session, we will dive into how to help your teams build reliable agentic applications, while taking a deliberate approach to context engineering. You will learn how to model information with explicit relationships, how to design retrieval patterns for agents that follow knowledge pathways, and how to build memory systems that preserve the connections sophisticated agentic reasoning demands.

title: Managing Small Datasets Without Losing Your Mind
description:
Many teams have to work with datasets that evolve over time. While it often starts as a simple setup, it quickly turns into a serious problem once dataset updates become regular.

It becomes painful to find the right methods inside a set of strangely named scripts that were obvious a month ago but not anymore. Scattered Python files and constant attempts to remember what to run — and in what order — to prepare new data, validate it, and retrain the model. Sounds familiar?

In this talk, I want to share what happened when dataset management was split into clear and understandable stages, each represented by a dedicated Python script. This workflow was developed in production and used for two years on datasets ranging from 2,000 to 200,000 images, with regular updates and model retraining cycles.

We split “obvious” operations into small scripts with clear boundaries, such as data collection, annotation, validation, merging, training, and evaluation. This allows the team to simply follow the same steps on each dataset update and greatly reduce cognitive load.

The talk mainly focuses on datasets used for object detection, segmentation, and OCR, but the patterns discussed can be applied to most file-based datasets used in Python data projects.

title: Trust Issues: Because Zero-Trust Isn’t Optional Anymore
description:
“Just ship it” doesn’t cut it anymore, everything in our stack is connected, exposed, and talking to things it probably shouldn’t. Modern applications don’t just benefit from Zero-Trust principles; they depend on them. And yet… our frontends trust too much, our backends trust too much, and our infrastructure trusts everything by default. With attacks like React2Shell emerging from seemingly harmless origins, we need to be prepared to minimize the impact of inevitable vulnerabilities.

In this talk, I break down Zero-Trust in a practical, full stack way - from the browser all the way down to the infrastructure.

The talk starts at the Frontend, where simple changes like strong CSPs, proper handling of CSRF tokens, secure cookies, and escaping strategies can prevent entire classes of attacks before they ever reach your server. Then we move into the Backend, exploring security patterns that make your services resilient even when assumptions fail, because they will. This includes approaches like mounting secrets via files instead of environment variables, enforcing permission-based API access, and more.

Finally, we zoom out to the Infrastructure layer, where Zero-Trust becomes real: mTLS for identity, distroless and unprivileged containers to shrink the attack surface, network cuts and segmentation to isolate blast zones, and other future-proof operational patterns that keep your system secure even when (not if) something goes sideways.

By the end of the talk, the audience walks away with a practical, full-stack security playbook everyone can apply immediately: from browser headers to Docker containers. Just the tools you need to build systems that don’t rely on trust, assumptions, or wishful thinking.

Zero-Trust isn’t optional anymore. But with the right patterns, it also doesn’t have to be painful.

title: You say you’ve got an AI SDLC. So which one?
description:
The session will explore not just one but three different organizational strategies of adopting AI.

The thing is, your organization has likely been on a specific trajectory of its evolution before the GenAI and agentic wave hit.

So likely with AI as an accelerator, your organization is still operating in the same paradigm, guided by its historical management system.

But was that trajectory right one in the first place? And does it stand in the face of tomorrow’s challenges?

Many organizations spread AI as fairy’s dust, giving each employee GPT licenses and a token budget. But does it result in global business-level performance? It surely does add costs.

In this session, powered by Org Topologies—a body of work on modern organizational design—Alexey will explore three AI adoption strategies. This will allow you and your leaders to challenge your organization’s status quo. And this can open the door to 10Xing your org-wide performance, not just making things locally faster.

title: Creative Engineering: Becoming the Leader You Want to Be
description: In this fireside chat, Pragya reflects on her journey from individual contributor to tech lead. Using a “creative engineering” mindset, she shares how experimentation, feedback loops, and iteration helped her evolve her leadership style: adapting to different people, embracing uncertainty, and leading effectively through change. Together, we’ll also discuss how to decide what’s flexible–and what’s non-negotiable–when building diverse teams and culture. This conversation is for anyone stepping into leadership, expanding scope, or rethinking their role in an evolving tech landscape.

title: Models Gone Rogue: Securing AI Integrations
description:
AI integrations are becoming mainstream in web applications through simple-looking API calls. But these integrations introduce new risks that break traditional security assumptions. As developers, we sit at the gateway for these vulnerabilities, and we are in a unique position to prevent them before they reach production.

In this session, we will walk through what calling an AI API looks like in practice and why natural language input and unpredictable outputs expand the attack surface, before exploring four real-world vulnerability categories:

1. Prompt Injection: when users manipulate AI into changing its behaviour.

2. Malicious Output: why we can't blindly trust what AI models return.

3. Denial of Wallet: how unbounded calls can drain API budgets overnight.

4. Data Spillage: when sensitive information leaks out of context.

For each, you will see concrete attack scenarios illustrated with visuals, followed by practical, developer-friendly mitigations with code examples drawn from hands-on experience building AI integrations.

Throughout the session, we'll return to a central idea: no single defence is enough. Securing AI integrations requires a defence-in-depth approach, multiple layers working together to reduce risk and limit impact.

You'll leave with practical security patterns you can apply today as AI becomes a permanent part of the web stack.

title: Modern Game Development Workflow: AI-Driven Productivity for Tech Teams
description:
The talk will examine practical, production-ready changes in workflow and design, with a focus on widely used tools such as Unity and Cursor. I will discuss how AI reshapes prototyping, implementation, and testing practices, particularly how approaches like dependency injection, test-driven development (TDD), and contemporary software design patterns evolve when AI copilots are embedded into the development lifecycle.

Attendees, including backend, frontend, and DevOps & cloud engineers, will gain insight into how AI accelerates iteration cycles, improves code robustness, supports architectural decision-making, and enhances cross-functional collaboration between technical and non-technical stakeholders.

The session offers practical advice on how to use AI tools in game development processes to boost team productivity, make delivery smoother, and keep engineering practices up-to-date in a fast-changing industry.

title: AI Discovery in product teams: how to think and validate with AI Workshop
description:
AI Discovery in product teams: how to think and validate with AI by Marta Strykowska from Launchpads AI

In this hands on masterclass, you will experience how vibe coding can accelerate iteration cycles, improve cross team alignment, and speed up validation to reach real business outcomes and build better products.

This workshop is grounded in practical experience consulting companies, coaching individuals to level up their vibe coding skills, and building my own startup while closely observing AI native teams. These teams operate differently: they shorten feedback loops, validate earlier, and use AI as an active collaborator across product, design, and engineering.

We will get straight into a concrete framework for AI driven discovery and validation. Together, we will use vibe coding tools to move from problem framing to a working prototype, showing how this approach supports faster decision making, clearer collaboration, and less waste across teams. This is not about replacing existing workflows, but about evolving them.

Participants will receive extra credits for one of the vibe coding tools and actively build during the session. You will leave with a tangible prototype or live project, and we will conclude with reflections on what the modern, AI native Product Manager role is really about.

title: AI for Everything After Code: Ship Fast, Stay Safe
description:
AI for Everything After Code: Ship Fast, Stay Safe

Most teams have “done DevOps” and “built a platform,” but still wrestle with the same core problems: platforms that developers dodge, AI that accelerates coding while quietly degrading delivery performance, security and compliance that can’t keep up, cloud bills that keep climbing, and incident response that hasn’t caught up with cloud‑native complexity.

We’ll move beyond the pain points to explore the 'After Scenario': treating your internal platform as a high-performance product. We will define what ‘good’ looks like in practice—where opinionated golden paths and invisible security defaults become the standard. By leveraging AI to reduce cognitive load and baking reliability into every change, we’ll use Harness as the reference implementation for a governed, cost-aware, and AI-native developer experience.

Three key takeaways

Platform as product: Why successful platform teams focus on golden paths, adoption, and DX, not just Kubernetes and Backstage.
Governed, AI‑enabled delivery: How Harness bakes security, compliance, and AI‑assisted testing/operations into the pipeline without slowing teams down.`
Unified value stream: How consolidating CI/CD, DevEx, security, SRE, and FinOps on a single platform lets you improve velocity, resilience, security, and cost in one go.

title: My Context Progress: A Psychologist's journey to implementing an MCP
description: As AI agents move from demos to real workflows, every API needs to become agent-friendly. This talk covers the path from prompt hacks to the Model Context Protocol and how it made Storyblok’s CMS accessible to AI agents. Learn how OpenAPI overlays helped tame a large API surface, what goes into building an MCP server, and why a background in psychology helps when reasoning about context - for both machines and humans. Whether you’re a backend developer, CMS architect, or exploring agentic AI, you’ll leave understanding how to expose your own API to AI agents.

title: It's Time To Talk About Signals
description:
With the active TC39 proposal in play, I think it’s about time we talk about Signals.

Over the years, the concept has had many different names. From “observables” in KnockoutJS, to “refs” in VueJS, before Solid made “signals” popular. We’ve seen the carcinization of frontend frameworks in the pursuit of more seamless and fine-grained reactivity in our applications.

But what actually are Signals? Why do we keep coming back to them? And why is their potential adoption as a language standard such a big deal?

title: The Industry Standard hoax: How engineers can steer product decisions
description:
The concept of "industry standard" in SaaS is often a hoax—a powerful, misused phrase that pushes product decisions based on false authority rather than data and proper product fit. While standards matter, blindly adopting norms risks creating technical and product debt that is misaligned with customer needs.

This talk aims to empower Product Engineers—the essential technical sanity check for product decisions—to challenge decisions and move from passive implementers to active decision-makers. Attendees will learn a practical, three-step framework to vet any "industry standard" proposal. By applying it, engineers can ensure their time is spent on creating actual customer value.

title: Panel: Code vs. Compliance: Can Europe Build Without Breaking Itself?
description:
The EU has the ambition and the talent. It also has the AI Act, the DSA, the DMA, GDPR, and a regulatory landscape that leaves some founders questioning whether Europe is still the right place to build and scale, let alone succeed.

Can regulation drive innovation, or is it holding it back?

In this panel, we bring together the Brussels and startup perspectives to unpack what legislation actually means for companies trying to build and grow in Europe today. We’ll dive into the real friction points founders run into, from compliance complexity to the challenges of scaling across borders with different rules within Europe. Through real-world examples, we’ll also look at where policy helps, and where it creates barriers and needs immediate change.

Bios:

Daniel Friedlaender

Daniel is a political strategist with over 20 years of experience navigating the intersection of tech, policy, and business across the EU, UK, and globally. A specialist in digital innovation and IP, he bridges the gap between high-level political intelligence and geopolitics with practical business impacts.

Masha Moisseyeva

Masha is dedicated to helping startups go global. Through DutchBasecamp and her startup Ogni, she turns the challenges of EU compliance into a roadmap for expansion. With a decade of experience working with founders, VCs, and governments, she focuses on helping companies scale without burning runway.

Mark Wolkenfelt

Marcus is a Dutch founder building companies on both sides of regulation. At LeadLabel, a B2B data intelligence company serving telecom, energy and finance, he deals with the daily friction of GDPR and enterprise compliance. At Enclio, an AI ecological monitoring company, he operates in a market supported by biodiversity regulation. He speaks from experience on when policy creates markets, and when it starts to work against the people trying to build them.

title: All your AI are belong to us!
description:
AI has rapidly become embedded in nearly every part of modern software and daily workflows, from writing code and emails to powering entire product features. But as organizations race to integrate AI capabilities, they also inherit new, and often underestimated, attack surfaces. Many long-standing security vulnerabilities now appear in AI-flavored forms, and new classes of threats are emerging where traditional safeguards fall short.

This talk explores how AI systems can be manipulated, compromised, or exploited both from the perspective of end users and those building AI-enabled products. We’ll examine where AI pipelines create opportunities for attackers, how seemingly harmless inputs can evolve into harmful behavior, and the subtle ways models can be steered or corrupted. Finally, we’ll discuss practical strategies for designing AI features with security in mind and for staying resilient against adversarial misuse.

title: React: What Your AI-Written Frontend Really Looks Like
description:
AI is already generating pull requests, but what would your codebase look like if you quietly let it keep going for six months? This talk tours an imaginary product built almost entirely by AI and inspects the code left behind: flawless boilerplate, exhaustive tests, beautiful Tailwind utilities… wrapped around a shaky architecture that nobody quite understands.​

Using concrete, code-level examples, the talk contrasts what AI excels at in frontend work, like CRUD scaffolds, syntax and API lookup, test and CSS snippet generation, with the places it consistently struggles, like architectural consistency, state management choices, performance trade‑offs, and design system coherence. Attendees will see how “AI-native” projects drift toward duplicated patterns, mismatched abstractions, and brittle state, even while velocity metrics look great.​​

I’ll end with opinionated guidelines for teams about how to let AI handle the boring parts while humans guard architecture, performance, and UX, and how to review AI-generated code so “What your code would look like if AI wrote it” becomes a cautionary tale, not your production repo.​

title: Agents at Work: How GitLab Is Redefining AI Adoption for Engineering Teams
description: Moving beyond scattered AI tools toward workflows where agents genuinely take work off your team's plate — that's where the real productivity shift happens. In this session, you'll see how GitLab Duo Agent Platform makes that possible through intelligent, event-driven flows where multiple AI agents collaborate on complex tasks end to end.

title: ONLINE ONLY WORKSHOP: From Idea to Production with AI
description:
From Idea to Production with AI

By unlearn.dev - Most AI workflows start with a blank prompt and end with code you have to fix. This one starts with a structured spec that removes every ambiguity before the agent writes a single line. You'll write a spec, watch an agent build from it live, then do it yourself.

Watch a full product get built live from a single OpenSpec file, no prompt engineering, no back-and-forth
Learn the propose, apply, and archive loop that turns vague ideas into specs agents can't misread
See what happens when you add guardrails mid-build and how one constraint changes everything the agent produces
Spec and build a feature yourself with your own agent in a 15-minute hands-on exercise

title: Simplicity in Complexity
description: In this session, dive into the art of making seemingly complex business rules translate seamlessly into simple, user-friendly code blocks. Learn practical strategies and techniques for streamlining the development process while involving business logic. Discover how to strike the perfect balance between functionality and simplicity, ensuring your apps not only meet intricate business requirements but also provide an intuitive and enjoyable user experience.

title: Beyond Websites: Future-Proofing Digital Experiences for Real Spaces
description:
For years, most digital experiences have lived behind screens - but the next wave of the web is moving into real environments: shops, galleries, hotels and public spaces.

As a developer with 25+ years in the industry, from leading large digital teams at a WPP agency to founding my own studio, I’m increasingly focused on how JavaScript, 3D and creative technology can extend beyond the traditional browser.

This talk explores how engineers can future-proof their skills as digital becomes more spatial, interactive and integrated with physical environments.

﻿I’ll cover the fundamentals that remain essential, the areas evolving fastest, how to approach prototyping for physical-digital work, and why this shift matters for the next chapter of our industry.

title: AI-Native: The Next Revolution after Cloud Native
description:
Cloud-native has redefined our approach to building and scaling software—from containers to microservices.

Today, AI is about to trigger an even more radical transformation. This revolution goes beyond new technologies; it calls for a complete rethinking of our software, architectural design, and even organizational collaboration.

Although the AI-native horizon is still forming, the promise of groundbreaking change is undeniable. Join me as I explore new challenges as well as endless possibilities.

title: Building High-IdQ Teams
description:
Your team has the talent. Your stack is solid. So why do decisions still stall, ideas get lost, or some voices dominate while others go quiet?

In fast-moving AI and engineering environments, the biggest constraint isn't technical — it's the kind of intelligence your team has, and the kind it's missing.

In this session, Ingrid Tappin introduces Identity Intelligence (IdQ™️) — a new leadership framework drawn from her book Exceptionals. You'll see why teams default to a narrow band of leadership instincts, what gets lost when they do, and the four identity-shaped intelligences that make teams sharper, faster, and more adaptive.

You'll leave with a way to read your own team that you can't unsee — and a free assessment that maps your own IdQ in five minutes.

If you're building AI products, scaling engineering teams, or leading through constant change, this session will give you a new lens on what's actually slowing you down.

title: Rust-Inspired TypeScript
description: Rust is rapidly becoming the backbone of modern JavaScript tooling. At the same time, its core principles around safety, correctness, and performance offer invaluable lessons for how we design and maintain large-scale TypeScript codebases. This talk explores what the JS ecosystem is already gaining from Rust, what JS/TS developers can learn from Rust’s language and design, and how those ideas translate into practical techniques you can adopt today.

title: Measuring AI Adoption Across People & Orgs (Maturity Model)
description:
There's a big difference between your team using ChatGPT occasionally and your organization truly adopting AI. I will share a practical framework to measure real AI maturity across individuals, teams, and entire organizations, with honest benchmarks, common pitfalls, and a clear roadmap for what comes next.

Additional topics:

1. Software Quality Playbook — 50+ Projects Framework

2. Agile is Slow for AI — How We Adapted Scrum

3. Hidden Risks of AI-Assisted Coding (Security, Side-effects)

title: Networking Mixer: Diverse Leaders In Tech
description:
Join the Networkign Mixer from Diverse Leaders In Tech at the Networking Square in the Main hall.

Driving Real DEI Change in Tech

Diverse Leaders in Tech (DLIT) helps tech companies measure, improve and lead in diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI).

Measure & Strengthen Your DEI Strategy

In order to improve you first need to measure. The DEI Scan gives you a data-driven view of your company’s DEI efforts. By highlighting strengths, identifying gaps, and outlining clear next steps, you’ll be able to drive real change. The best part? It only takes 15 minutes.

Start the free DEI Scan here: https://diverseleadersintech.typeform.com/DEIbenchmark?typeform-source=diverseleadersintech.com

title: Self-Organizing Tech Teams - Without Overhead or AI Overlords
description:
Self-organizing, co-ownership, and crowd-sourcing ideas are the future in the age of AI.

﻿

But why haven't we replaced our bosses yet?

This talk goes beyond a naive "AI CEO", but introduces a vision for myriad of AI-powered tools that makes 'collective intelligence' practical:

Collaborative vision and roadmap - without death-by-voting
Accountability systems that track and nudge - without surveillance
Flexible roles and rules - without chaos

At AIBuilders.club we're building this - starting with a super simple product that we use ourselves: meetups and community tools

title: Decoding JavaScript: Loading Performance
description: Speed is paramount in the world of Web apps. This talk will show how we transformed a slow dashboard into a lightning-fast command center. We’ll delve into the “toolbox of loading performance” and practical solutions to common issues. You’ll learn how shipping less code, delivering smarter, and aggressive caching can slash initial load times and reduce global latency, making repeat visits instant. The talk will also cover the new frontiers in performance, such as Vite, Esbuild, and HTTP/3. Performance is about more than just bundle size; it’s about the entire user experience. We’ll show you how we made a powerful dashboard instantly fast.

title: What the F is Going On? Cognition is Cheap, Understanding and Judgement is Not.
description:
Every generation of engineering managers inherits a calculus - a set of intuitions about what's scarce, what's expensive, and what's worth optimizing. Hire slowly. Review carefully. Protect your seniors' time. Most of these intuitions were forged when cognition itself was the scarce resource: writing code, weighing options, summarizing a doc, scanning a codebase - all of it took a human brain and an afternoon.

That assumption is now broken.

title: Under the Hood of Undici: Implementing HTTP/2 for the Modern Web
description:
For years, Node.js developers relied on the legacy node:http module, as performance requirements grew, its limitations like clunky event-emitter patterns, and high memory overhead became apparent. Enter Undici: a high-performance HTTP/1.1 (and now HTTP/2) client written from scratch for Node.js.

In this session, we’ll peel back the layers of Undici’s architecture to understand how it achieves up to more throughput than traditional clients. We’ll explore:

The Internals: How Undici replaces the overhead of http.Agent with a more efficient connection pool and dispatcher model.
Powering Native fetch: A look at how Undici serves as the engine behind the global fetch API in Node.js, bringing browser-standard networking to the server with zero external dependencies.
The HTTP/2 Leap: The technical journey of implementing HTTP/2 support, including the transition from simple request-response cycles to binary framing, multiplexing, and concurrent streams.
Real-World Benefits: Why shifting to an HTTP/2-capable client matters for modern microservices.

Whether you're a performance enthusiast or just curious about the future of the Node.js internals, join us to see how we're rebuilding the network stack for the modern web.

title: AI Org Change Management: Team Transition to the Agentic SDLC and Ways of Working
Networking Mixer: Leaders in Tech & Founders
description:
For all the Leaders In Tech out there!

Come and join us for the Tech Leadership Networking Mixer at Devworld Conference. Connect with fellow CTO's, Engineering Managers, Tech Leads, Founders, CEO's and Engineering VPs whilst enjoying a drink on us!

title: Beyond Bugs: How AI Turns QA into Full-Cycle Engineers
description:
The role of a QA engineer is expanding. With AI agents gaining new capabilities through the Model Context Protocol (MCP), testers can leverage them to go beyond detecting defects to reproducing them, pinpointing root causes, and implementing validated fixes. This is making it possible for QA professionals to work across design, development, and testing in ways that were once the domain of separate specialized roles. The traditional boundaries between QA, frontend, backend, and automation engineers are becoming less relevant as AI handles much of the heavy lifting, letting individuals focus on decision-making and delivering quality.

In this talk, we will look at how MCP-enabled AI can become a partner throughout the entire product lifecycle, from the earliest design conversations to validating production releases. You will see how the same workflows that speed up bug detection can also improve coverage, accelerate delivery, and expand the scope of what a single engineer can achieve. Along the way, we will explore what this shift means for building adaptable, cross-skilled teams and for rethinking the way QA and development collaborate in an AI-driven world.
